title: Answers to Week 3 Hands On Exercise 2 Logistic Regression Python INFS3822
body:
INFS3822 – AI for Business Analytics

Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Exercise 2 – Python Logistic Regression to predict Customer Churn This is a step-by-step Guide to apply Logistic Regression Algorithm to conduct structured data analysis in Python. By completing these activities, you will be able to build a logistic regression model to predict Customer Churn.

• Objectives o Build a logistic regression model in Python to predict customer churn. • Data: A CSV file of online retail customers including whether a customer churned or not, and their total spend.

Please note the step-by-step Guide to apply linear regression model is provided in a separate document uploaded on Moodle under section Week3.

1. Load the dependencies First, we need to load the necessary libraries. These libraries help manipulate data, split data into training and testing sets, build models, and assess the accuracy of the performance of the models.

• The pandas library, which is used for data manipulation and analysis. • The train_test_split function to split the data into training and testing sets. • The LogisticRegression class, which is a linear model for binary classification • The accuracy_score function which is used to measure/assess the performance of a model.

import pandas as pd import numpy as np from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error from sklearn import preprocessing import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

2. Load the data Next, we need to load our dataset. To do so, you can download the dataset from Moodle as a CSV file called ‘data_online_retail_customers.csv’ and upload it to your Colab notebook. Let's load the dataset from the csv file provided as follows: # Load the data data = pd.read_csv("data_online_retail_customers.csv") CRICOS Provider Code 00098G

The code data = pd.read_csv("data_online_retail_customers.csv") reads the data from CSV file 'data_online_retail_customers.csv' and l stores it in a variable called data using the read_csv function from the pandas library (aliased as pd). 3. Clean the data In this step we need to check if there are missing values. # Check for missing values missing_values = data.isna() print(missing_values.any().any()) This dataset does not include any missing values. This is demonstrated by False in the result of the code. In case the above code produces True, this means that the dataset includes missing values and you need address that using the following code. # Remove rows with missing values data = data.dropna()

4. Check Data Types We need to check the data types of all variables in our dataset. # Print the data type of all variables print(data.dtypes)

You can notice that some columns, like Gender and Promotion_Response, are of type ‘object’ (text). We need to convert text data into categorical variable.

5. Data transformation In this step, we need to conduct data transformation to convert text data (shown as type object) into numeric or categorical data. e.g. Gender and Promotion_Response should be categorical. You can do that as follows:

# Convert categorical columns to 'category' dtype data['Gender'] = data['Gender'].astype('category') data['Promotion_Response'] = data['Promotion_Response'].astype('category')

Now that text data is transformed into categorical data, you can have a look at the data type of all variables in the dataset to check that the transformation has been applied. Notice that Gender and Promotion_Response are now categorical variables.

# Print the data type of all variables

print(data.dtypes)

You would also need to convert categorical variables to dummy variables for use in the logistic regression late.

# Convert categorical variables to dummy variables data = pd.get_dummies(data, drop_first=True)

Now that categorical data is transformed into dummy variables, you can have a look at the data type of all variables in the dataset to check that the transformation has been applied.

# Print the data type of all variables print(data.dtypes)

6. Feature Selection and Splitting the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) to predict customer churn In this step, we will perform feature selection and split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y). Feature selection is crucial because it involves choosing the right variables (features) that will help us make accurate predictions. As we discussed in Week 2 seminar, feature selection allows to select relevant features (variables) to build the model. Feature selection improves the model's performance by removing irrelevant or less important features, reduce the complexity of the model, which can make it faster to train and easier to interpret, and preventing overfitting. We need to select the key variables that might affect the target variable ‘Customer Churn’ whether customers leave or stay. Why Exclude Customer_ID? In our dataset, we have a column called ‘Customer_ID’. This column is simply an identifier for each customer and does not provide any relevant information for predicting whether a customer will churn or

not. Including Customer_ID in our model would not make sense because it does not influence customer behaviour to churn or not churn. Therefore, we exclude it from our input features. For this step, exclude the ‘Customer_ID’ feature because it cannot predict churn. Customer_ID is an identifier, so it can't help predicting customer churn because it's just a unique identifier assigned to each customer. It doesn't contain any information about the customer's behaviour or characteristics that could influence whether they stay or leave. You can consider all other variables as inputs for your analysis and make sure that you conduct rigorous feature selection to identify and select the most important features for your predictive models. Likewise, the column Churned is our target variable, which indicates whether a customer has churned (left) or not. This column should not be included in the input features because it is what we are trying to predict. Now, let's split the data into two sets: Input features (X), these are the features we will use to make predictions. They include all the relevant variables except ‘Customer_ID’ and ‘Churned’. Output variable (y) which is the target variable we want to predict, which is ‘churned’. # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) X = data.drop(columns=['Customer_ID', 'Churned']) y = data["Churned"] The line ‘data.drop(columns=['Customer_ID', 'Churned'])’ removes the Customer_ID and Churned columns from the dataset. The remaining columns are stored in ‘X’ and will be used as the input features for our model. The line ‘data["Churned"]’ selects the ‘Churned’ column from the dataset and stores it in ‘y’. This will be our target variable, the one we aim to predict. Why Do This?

• By excluding ‘Customer_ID’, we ensure that our model is not using an irrelevant feature that could confuse the model. • By excluding ‘Churned’ from ‘X’, we make sure that the target variable is not part of the input features. Including the target variable in the input features would lead to data leakage, where the model could learn from the target variable directly instead of learning the underlying patterns.

Please refer to the Step-by-step Guide for Feature selection on Moodle under section Week 4, to conduct feature selection applied to the other variables to identify and select the most important variables needed to build the model. This step is crucial for building a robust and reliable model that can make accurate predictions. Now that we have selected relevant features and split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y), we need to preprocess the data. One important preprocessing step is scaling the data. What is Scaling Data? Scaling data means adjusting the range of features so that they have a standard scale. This is particularly important for ML algorithms that rely on the distances between data points, such as logistic regression and linear regression. Why is Scaling Important? It ensures that all features contribute equally to the model's predictions. Without scaling, features with larger ranges might dominate the learning process. It helps gradient-based optimisation algorithms converge faster. Models often perform better when the data is scaled. Some algorithms can be sensitive to large differences in the scale of data, leading to numerical stability issues.

7. Preprocess the data (scaling) One way to do scaling, to adjust the size of data is through Standardisation. This method makes sure all the numeric input features are on the same scale. It does this by subtracting the average value (mean) of the data and then dividing by how much the data varies (standard deviation). # Scale the numeric input data To scale the data, We will use the StandardScaler from the sklearn.preprocessing module to scale our data. Here's how to do it: The following line of code selects columns with numerical data types (float64 and int64) because these are the columns we need to scale. # Identify numeric columns numeric_columns = X.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64']).columns

‘preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X[numeric_columns])’: This line calculates the mean and standard deviation for each numeric feature. ‘X[numeric_columns] = scaler.transform(X[numeric_columns])’ This line scales the numeric features using the calculated mean and standard deviation. # Scale numeric columns scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X[numeric_columns]) X[numeric_columns] = scaler.transform(X[numeric_columns])

By scaling our data, we ensure that all features contribute equally to the model, improving its performance and stability. Now that we have scaled our data, we can move on to training our logistic regression model. 8. Split the data into training and testing sets Before we train our model, we need to split our data into two parts: training and testing sets. The training set will be used to train the model, while the testing set will be used to test the accuracy of the model; how well our model performs to make predictions.

# Split the data into training and testing sets X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

To split our data into training and testing sets, we use the function ‘train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)’ The ‘test_size=0.2’ parameter means that 20% of the data will be used for testing, and the remaining 80% will be used for training.

Note: After you split the data into training and testing datasets, you conduct feature selection to select the most important variables for your model. For details please refer to the step-by-step Feature selection in Python/R.

9. Train the Logistic Regression Model on the training data Now that we have our training and testing sets, we can create a logistic regression model and train it using the training data. Let's Create a logistic regression model and train it using the training dataset # Train a logistic regression model on the training data model = LogisticRegression() model.fit(X_train, y_train)

‘model = LogisticRegression()’ creates an instance of the Logistic Regression model. ‘model.fit(X_train, y_train)’ trains the logistic regression model using the training data (X_train and y_train).

10. Make predictions on the testing data After training our model, we need to see how well it performs on the testing data. We do this by making predictions. # Make predictions on the testing data y_pred = model.predict(X_test)

The function ‘model.predict (X_test)’ is used to predict the outcome of the testing data (‘X_test’).

11. Calculate the accuracy of the model Next, we calculate the accuracy of our model by comparing the predictions with the true values in ‘y_test’. # Calculate the accuracy of the model accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred) print("Accuracy:", accuracy) Accuracy: 0.47058823529411764 The line ‘accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)’ calculates the accuracy of the model by comparing the true labels (y_test) with the predicted labels (y_pred). The line ‘print("Accuracy:", accuracy)’ displays the calculated accuracy.

How to interpret Accuracy Score for customer churn? Accuracy measures the proportion of correct predictions (true results both true positives and true negatives) among the total number of cases examined. An accuracy of 0.47058823529411764 implies that the model correctly predicts the outcome approximately 47.06% of the time- the model is correct about 47 times out of 100.This means that around 52.94% of the predictions made by the model are incorrect. In a simple way, we could say that if the model makes 100 predictions, about 47 of the predictions will be correct, while 53 of predictions will be incorrect. In this case, the accuracy indicates how often the model is right in its predictions, both when it predicts that a customer will churn (and they do) and when it predicts that a customer will not churn (and they don’t).

What does this mean for customer churn?

• True Positives (TP): Correctly predicts a customer will churn. • True Negatives (TN): Correctly predicts a customer will not churn. • False Positives (FP): Incorrectly predicts a loyal customer will leave. • False Negatives (FN): Incorrectly predicts a churning customer will stay.

Current Model Performance

• 47.06% Accuracy: Correctly identifies churn and non-churn customers in about 47 out of 100 cases. • 52.94% Errors: Makes incorrect predictions in about 53 out of 100 cases, including both false positives and false negatives.

What does this mean True Positives and True Negatives?

True Positives (TP): These are cases where the model correctly predicts that a customer will churn. For example, if a customer is indeed going to leave the company and the model predicts this correctly, it is a true positive.

True Negatives (TN): These are cases where the model correctly predicts that a customer will not churn. For example, if a customer is going to stay with the company and the model predicts this correctly, it is a true negative.

So, when we say our model correctly identifies nearly half of the cases it examines, it means that in approximately 47.06% of the cases, the model correctly identifies both the customers who will leave (true positives) and those who will stay (true negatives).

What does it mean False Positives and False Negatives?

False Positives (FP): These are cases where the model incorrectly predicts that a customer will churn when they actually will not. For example, if a loyal customer is predicted by the model to leave, it is a false positive.

False Negatives (FN): These are cases where the model incorrectly predicts that a customer will not churn when they actually will. For example, if a customer is going to leave the company but the model predicts they will stay, it is a false negative.

When we say our model makes incorrect predictions for more than half of the cases, it means that in about 52.94% of the cases, the model incorrectly predicts customer behaviour. This includes cases where:

• The model predicts a customer will leave, but they stay (false positive). • The model predicts a customer will stay, but they leave (false negative).

Why This Matters? Having an accuracy of 47.06% means that the model's predictions are not very reliable. This low accuracy indicates that the model needs improvement because it is wrong more often than it is right. Here's why improving this is important:

• Business Decisions: Accurate predictions help allocate resources effectively. Incorrect predictions can waste resources on unnecessary retention efforts or miss opportunities to retain customers. If a model predicts that a loyal customer will leave, the company might unnecessarily spend resources trying to retain them (false positive). Conversely, if a model fails to identify a customer who is about to leave, the company misses the chance to retain them (false negative). • Customer Satisfaction: Correct predictions enhance customer satisfaction by targeting the right customers with appropriate actions. Incorrect predictions can lead to poor customer service and satisfaction. For example, a customer who is wrongly predicted to churn might receive unnecessary retention offers, which could be annoying. • Resource Allocation: Ensures investments are targeted correctly, optimizing marketing and customer service efforts. Accurate predictions help in better allocation of resources, ensuring efforts and investments are targeted correctly.

Therefore, we can summarise:

True Positives and True Negatives: Our model correctly identifies nearly half of the cases. This includes accurately predicting both customers who will churn and those who will not.

False Positives and False Negatives: The model makes incorrect predictions for more than half of the cases. This includes predicting that a customer will churn when they won’t (false positive) and predicting that a customer will not churn when they will (false negative).

With an accuracy of 47.06%, it is clear that the model's predictions may not be reliable enough for making critical business decisions without further tuning and enhancement. Improving the model will lead to more accurate predictions, better business decisions, and higher customer satisfaction.

Precision, Recall and F1 Score to assess the performance of the model in making predictions While accuracy is a relevant metric, it is not comprehensive to assess the performance of the model. The metrics Precision, Recall, and F1 score provide more insights into the model's performance. Each of these metrics provides different insights into the model's performance, particularly in terms of how well the model is identifying the relevant instances (cases) of customer churn.

from sklearn.metrics import precision_score, recall_score, f1_score # Calculate precision, recall, and F1 score precision = precision_score(y_test, y_pred, average='macro') # or 'micro', 'weighted' depending on your need recall = recall_score(y_test, y_pred, average='macro') f1 = f1_score(y_test, y_pred, average='macro')

print(f"Precision: {precision}") print(f"Recall: {recall}") print(f"F1 Score: {f1}")

Precision: 0.4698847575559904 Recall: 0.47004109885355827 F1 Score: 0.4695557963163597

How to interpret the Precision Score for customer churn? Precision is a metric that indicates how accurate the positive predictions made by the model are. It is the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to the total predicted positives. High precision means that the model has a low false positive rate. Precision answers the question: "Of all the instances that the model predicted as positive (customer will churn), how many were actually positive? (customers actually churned)?"

• High Precision: Indicates that the model has a low false positive rate, which means that the model rarely predicts that a customer will churn when they actually won't. • Low Precision: Indicates that the model has a high false positive rate, which means that the model often predicts that a customer will churn when they actually won't.

The results of the Logistic regression show that the model has a precision score of 0.4698847575559904 -approximately 47%. This means that out of all the customers that the model predicted would churn, only 47% actually did. This means that the model is not very accurate in predicting customer churn, as more than half of the positive predictions (customers predicted to churn) are incorrect.

Let’s interpret further the result; the Precision measures the accuracy of the model's positive predictions. The Precision Score: 0.4698847575559904 indicates that 47% of the customers predicted to churn actually did. So, out of 100 customers predicted to churn, only 47 actually churned. The remaining 53 were incorrectly predicted to churn, indicating that the model has a relatively high number of false positives.

• Predicted Positives (Customers Predicted to Churn): 100 • True Positives (Customers Actually Churned): 47 • False Positives (Customers Predicted to Churn but Didn't): 53

The model could be improved further, because more than half of its positive predictions are incorrect, leading to a high false positive rate. Improving precision would mean making more accurate predictions about which customers will actually churn, thereby reducing the number of false positives and improving the overall reliability of the model.

How to interpret the Recall Score for customer churn? Recall (also known as Sensitivity or True Positive Rate) measures the ability of the model to find all the relevant instances in the dataset- to identify all actual churn cases. Specifically, it is the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to all the actual positives. In simple way, we could say that recall answers the question: "Of all the customers who actually churned, how many did the model correctly predict would churn?"

• High Recall: Indicates that the model correctly identifies most of the actual churn cases. • Low Recall: Indicates that the model misses many of the actual churn cases.

Our logistic model has a Recall score of 0.47004109885355827 or approximately 47%. This indicates that that the model correctly identifies 47% of the customers who actually churned. Which means out of all the customers who actually churned, the model correctly predicted about 47% of them. This means that the model fails to identify the remaining 53% of customers who actually churned.

Let’s illustrate with an example, if Actual Churned Customers is 100. The Correctly Predicted Churned Customers (True Positives) would be 47, and the Missed Churned Customers (False Negatives) would be 53. In this example, out of 100 customers who actually churned, the model correctly identified 47 of them. The remaining 53 customers who actually churned were not correctly identified by the model, meaning they were missed. The model could be improved in finding all the customers who are at risk of churning. With a recall of 47%, the model is missing over half of the customers who actually churn, which could lead to missed opportunities for customer retention efforts. How to interpret the F1 Score for customer churn? The F1 Score is a metric that combines Precision and Recall into a single number. It is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, which means it balances the two metrics. This is especially useful when you want to find a good balance between correctly predicting customers who will churn (Recall) and making sure that your predictions are accurate (Precision). The F1 Score is particularly important when you have an imbalanced dataset, where the number of customers who churn is much smaller than those who don't. Our logistic model has an F1 Score of 0.4695557963163597 or approximately 47%. The F1 Score combines both the Precision and Recall of the model. An F1 Score of 47% indicates that the model is balanced in terms of its ability to identify actual churn cases and the accuracy of its positive predictions. Let’s explain with an example; Precision captures How many of the customers predicted to churn actually did (e.g., 47% precision). Recall captures How many of the actual churned customers were correctly identified (e.g., 47% recall). F1 Score Combines these two to generate a single measure of the model's performance. In a simple way, we could say that an F1 Score of 47% means that the model's performance in predicting customer churn is moderate, balancing between identifying those who will churn and ensuring the predictions are accurate.

Why is the F1 Score Important?

• Balance: It shows how well the model performs overall, considering both Precision and Recall. • Imbalanced Datasets: It's particularly useful when the dataset is imbalanced, meaning there are significantly more non-churned customers than churned ones. In such cases, looking at just accuracy might be misleading.

In summary, F1 Score measures the balance between Precision and Recall. F1 Score of 0.4695557963163597 indicates that the model has a moderate balance, correctly predicting churn with moderate accuracy and identifying actual churn cases moderately well. The model's predictions are not perfect and there is room for improvement. A higher F1 Score would mean a better balance between making accurate predictions and correctly identifying all cases of customer churn. Improving the F1 Score would help in making more reliable and balanced predictions about which customers are at risk of leaving, thereby allowing businesses to implement effective customer retention strategies. Generate the results of the logistic regression model In this step we generate the coefficients of features to figure out which features in our data are most important for our machine learning model. This code will display the coefficients of the features used in the logistic regression model to predict whether a customer will churn based on various factors such as age, annual income, total spend, and more. We want to see which features had the most influence on the predictions. Display feature coefficients coefficients = pd.DataFrame({ 'Feature': X.columns, 'Coefficient': model.coef_[0] }) print(coefficients) We used pd.DataFrame to create a table (or DataFrame) that holds our feature names and their corresponding coefficients. Think of a DataFrame as a table with rows and columns, much like an Excel spreadsheet. The 'Feature' column lists the names of the features (or factors) we used to train our model. For example, features like 'Age', 'Annual Income', and 'Total Spend' are listed here. The 'Coefficient' column shows the values of the coefficients for each feature. Coefficients indicate how much each feature impacts the prediction. A positive coefficient means that as the feature increases, the likelihood of churn increases, while a negative coefficient means the opposite. When we run this code, we generate the following table:

Interpret the Coefficients and derive actionable insights

The coefficients in the table indicate how each feature impacts the likelihood of a customer churning. By understanding these coefficients, we can identify which factors are most important in predicting customer churn and take appropriate actions to address them. To determine if the variables are strong predictors of customer churn, we need to consider the context of the coefficient value relative to other features and the overall model. 'Total_Spend' Feature Let's determine if ''Total_Spend' (0.124728)’ is a strong predictor of customer churn by comparing it to the other features. Coefficient Value: The coefficient for 'Total_Spend' is 0.124728. This means that an increase in total spend is associated with an increase in the likelihood of customer churn. In other words, customers who spend more are more likely to leave. Magnitude: The absolute value of 0.124728 indicates the strength of the relationship between 'Total_Spend' and churn. To understand if this is a strong predictor, we need to compare it with the magnitudes of other coefficients. Relative Strength: Compared to other features, 'Total_Spend' has a higher absolute value than features like 'Age' (0.041073), 'Annual_Income' (-0.027079), and 'Satisfaction_Score' (0.020320). However, it is lower than 'Num_of_Purchases' (-0.164011) and 'Gender_Male' (-0.125998), but still significant in magnitude. Therefore, based on the coefficient value of 0.124728, 'Total_Spend' is a significant predictor / strong indicator of churn. A positive coefficient means that higher total spend is associated with a higher likelihood of churn. A plausible explanation for this finding could be that customers who spend more money might have higher expectations or might be using the service more intensively, which could lead to dissatisfaction if their expectations are not met or if they encounter issues. We might focus on customers with high total spend and offer them special incentives to retain them. Since 'Total_Spend' is a strong predictor, we recommend the business to regularly monitor highspending customers to identify signs of dissatisfaction. Implement a proactive approach to address their needs and concerns before they decide to churn. In addition, offer personalised incentives and loyalty programs to high-spending customers. Tailor your communication and offers to their preferences to increase their satisfaction and loyalty. In addition, it would be great if they could collect feedback specifically from high-spending customers to understand what aspects of your service or product they value and what areas need improvement. Use this feedback to make targeted enhancements. Create VIP programs that provide additional benefits and exclusive services to high-spending customers. This can make them feel valued and less likely to leave. By focusing on high-spending customers and addressing their specific needs, you can reduce churn and enhance customer retention.

Num_of_Purchases' Feature

Let's determine if 'Num_of_Purchases' (-0.164011) is a strong predictor of customer churn by comparing it to the other features. To determine if 'Num_of_Purchases' is a strong predictor of customer churn, we need to consider the context of the coefficient value relative to other features and the overall model. Let's look at the coefficient value of -0.164011 and interpret its strength. Coefficient Value: The coefficient for 'Num_of_Purchases' is -0.164011. This means that an increase in the number of purchases is associated with a decrease in the likelihood of customer churn. In other words, customers who make more purchases are less likely to leave.

Magnitude: The magnitude of a coefficient indicates the strength of the relationship between the feature and the target variable (churn in this case). In general, a larger absolute value indicates a stronger predictor. The absolute value of -0.164011 is relatively high compared to other features. For example, it is higher than the coefficients for 'Age' (0.041073), 'Annual_Income' (-0.027079), and 'Satisfaction_Score' (0.020320). Relative Strength: When compared to other features, 'Num_of_Purchases' has one of the larger absolute coefficient values. This suggests that it is a relatively strong predictor of customer churn. For instance, it is stronger than 'Gender_Male' (-0.125998) and 'Total_Spend' (0.124728). Therefore, given its relatively high absolute value, 'Num_of_Purchases' (-0.164011) can be considered a strong indicator of customer churn. Customers who make more purchases are less likely to churn, indicating a strong negative relationship between the number of purchases and the likelihood of leaving the service. Actionable Insights: Since 'Num_of_Purchases' is a strong predictor, business can implement strategies that encourage customers to make more purchases. For example, we recommend implementing loyalty programs that reward customers for their frequent purchases, such as points for each purchase that can be redeemed for discounts or freebies. Provide personalised offers and recommendations to customers based on their purchase history to encourage repeat purchases. We can also suggest introducing subscription models that provide regular deliveries or services, encouraging consistent purchasing behavior. By leveraging the insights from 'Num_of_Purchases,' businesses can develop targeted strategies to enhance customer retention and reduce churn.

‘Gender_Male' Feature

Let's determine if 'Gender_Male' (-0.125998) is a strong predictor of customer churn by comparing it to the other features.

Coefficient Value: The coefficient for 'Gender_Male' is -0.125998. This means that being male is associated with a lower likelihood of customer churn. Male customers are less likely to leave compared to female customers.

Magnitude: The absolute value of -0.125998 indicates the strength of the relationship between being male and churn. To understand if this is a strong predictor, we need to compare it with the magnitudes of other coefficients. Relative Strength: Compared to other features, 'Gender_Male' has a higher absolute value than many features, such as 'Age' (0.041073), 'Annual_Income' (-0.027079), 'Satisfaction_Score' (0.020320), and 'Email_Opt_In' (-0.041324). It has a lower absolute value than 'Num_of_Purchases' (-0.164011) but is still significant in magnitude. Therefore, based on the coefficient value of -0.125998, 'Gender_Male' is a significant predictor of customer churn. It suggests that male customers are less likely to churn. While it may not be the single strongest predictor, it is among the important features influencing customer churn.

Actionable insights

Since 'Gender_Male' is a strong predictor of customer churn, we recommend investigating why female customers might be more likely to churn compared to male customers. Conduct surveys or focus groups to gather insights into their specific needs and concerns. In addition, develop marketing campaigns and

retention strategies that specifically target female customers, addressing their unique preferences and pain points. Another suggestion is to engage female customers through personalized communication and offers. Ensure that their customer journey is as seamless and satisfying as possible. In addition, it would be great if they could implement service improvements that could enhance the experience for female customers. This could involve better customer support, improved product features, or more tailored services. By focusing on these insights, you can develop targeted strategies to reduce churn among female customers and improve overall customer retention. Promotion_Response_Responded Feature

Let's determine if 'Promotion_Response_Responded' (-0.076918) is a strong predictor of customer churn by comparing it to the other features.

Coefficient Value: The coefficient for 'Promotion_Response_Responded' is -0.076918, which means that customers who respond to promotions are less likely to churn. Responding to promotions is associated with a lower likelihood of churn. Customers who respond to promotions are less likely to leave.

Magnitude: The absolute value of -0.076918 indicates the strength of the relationship between responding to promotions and churn. To determine if this is a strong predictor, we need to compare it with the magnitudes of other coefficients. Relative Strength: 'Promotion_Response_Responded' has a higher absolute value than features like 'Age' (0.041073), 'Annual_Income' (-0.027079), 'Satisfaction_Score' (0.020320), and 'Email_Opt_In' (- 0.041324). However, it has a lower absolute value compared to 'Num_of_Purchases' (-0.164011), 'Total_Spend' (0.124728), and 'Gender_Male' (-0.125998). Therefore, based on the coefficient value of -0.076918, 'Promotion_Response_Responded' is a significant predictor of customer churn. It suggests that customers who respond to promotions are less likely to leave. While it may not be the strongest predictor, it is still an important feature influencing churn. Actionable Insight Since 'Promotion_Response_Responded' is a significant predictor, we recommend retailers to develop and implement targeted promotion strategies to engage customers. Also, track which promotions receive the most responses and replicate those strategies to retain more customers. In addition, personalise promotional offers based on customer preferences and behaviour. Use data analytics to understand what types of promotions resonate most with different customer segments. Regularly send out promotions to keep customers engaged. Ensure that promotions are valuable and relevant to the customers' interests and needs. Track customers who respond to promotions and follow up with additional offers or rewards to maintain their engagement and loyalty. By leveraging the insights from 'Promotion_Response_Responded,' you can develop targeted strategies to reduce churn and improve customer retention. Num_of_Support_Contacts Feature

let's determine if 'Num_of_Support_Contacts' (0.047972) is a strong predictor of customer churn by comparing it to the other features. A higher number of support contacts is associated with a higher likelihood of churn. Frequent support contact might indicate unresolved issues leading to dissatisfaction.

Coefficient Value: The coefficient for 'Num_of_Support_Contacts' is 0.047972. This means that an increase in the number of support contacts is associated with an increase in the likelihood of customer churn. Magnitude: The absolute value of 0.047972 indicates the strength of the relationship between the number of support contacts and churn. To determine if this is a strong predictor, we need to compare it with the magnitudes of other coefficients. Relative Strength: 'Num_of_Support_Contacts' has a higher absolute value than features like 'Annual_Income' (-0.027079), 'Satisfaction_Score' (0.020320), 'Email_Opt_In' (-0.041324), and 'Age' (0.041073). However, it has a lower absolute value compared to features like 'Total_Spend' (0.124728), 'Num_of_Purchases' (-0.164011), 'Gender_Male' (-0.125998), and 'Promotion_Response_Responded' (-0.076918). Therefore, based on the coefficient value of 0.047972, 'Num_of_Support_Contacts' is a moderate predictor of customer churn. It suggests that customers who contact support more frequently are more likely to leave. While it is not the strongest predictor, it is still significant in understanding churn behaviour. Since 'Num_of_Support_Contacts' is a predictor of customer churn, we recommend the business to Analyse the reasons why customers are contacting support frequently. Identify common issues and work on resolving them effectively to reduce the need for support contacts. Also, implement proactive customer service measures, such as regular check-ins with customers and providing self-service options to address common issues before they escalate. Collect feedback from customers who contact support frequently to understand their pain points and improve the overall customer experience. Train support staff to handle customer issues efficiently and empathetically. Ensure that they have the resources and knowledge to resolve issues quickly. By focusing on these insights, you can develop targeted strategies to improve customer satisfaction and reduce churn. Email_Opt_In Feature
